[Tool: Read → 1: //! Inference-based vulnerability engine.
2: //!
3: //! The design principle (the lesson from hardcoded rule tables not scaling):
4: //! **separate the *property* from the *signature*.** A small fixed set of
5: //! property [`TemplateKind`]s is hardcoded; the per-codebase *signatures* —
6: //! which functions are sinks, guards, sources, sanitizers — are *inferred*
7: //! ([`PredicateSet`]) rather than listed. A "bug class" (IDOR, BAC, SSRF,
8: //! lossy-send) is then a template instantiated with an inferred predicate set,
9: //! not new engine code.
10: //!
11: //! Signatures are inferred by stacked mechanisms, each tagged with its
12: //! [`InferenceOrigin`] so confidence can be combined and audited:
13: //!
14: //! * **Frequency** — deviant-behavior mining over corpus consistency
15: //!   ([`mining`]). If almost every caller of a sink passes through some guard,
16: //!   the few that don't are anomalies, and the guard is *discovered* (not
17: //!   named). This is the rule-free core; see [`mining::mine_missing_guards`].
18: //! * **Name** — the `taint_naming` lexicon (source/sink-leaning identifiers).
19: //! * **History** — learned from fix-commits and tool-call traces.
20: //! * **Llm** — proposed by a model, then *verified against graph facts* before
21: //!   any finding is emitted.
22: //! * **Seed** — a small hardcoded anchor set to bootstrap (tier 0).

## What MDASH does vs. what codegraph-rs has

I read the vuln engine (`analysis/src/vuln/`), the CLI analyze surface, and the output paths. Here's the honest gap map.

### What you already have (the substrate MDASH brags about)
- **Cross-file reasoning** — the exact thing MDASH's marketing centers on ("no single file looks wrong"). You have the call graph, dominators, points-to, and interprocedural taint (`scan_unsanitized_flows` → `PointsToOracle`). The Wasmtime/double-free "spread across 3-6 files" class is what this substrate is *for*.
- **Rule-free inference** — `mine_missing_guards` (deviant-frequency), confidence fusion via noisy-OR (`LearnedStore`), multi-origin tagging (frequency/name/history/llm/seed). Philosophically this *is* MDASH's "invest in orchestration/validation layers, not the model" thesis (`vuln/mod.rs:1-26`).
- **A trust boundary for model output** — `GraphVerifier` (`vuln/classify.rs:60`) is designed to verify LLM role proposals against graph facts before emitting.

### What you're missing (ranked by leverage)

1. **No LLM/agent layer at all.** `PredicateClassifier` (`classify.rs:43`) has **zero implementations**; `GraphVerifier` has **zero callers**; `InferenceOrigin::Llm` is never produced in production. No `reqwest`/openai/anthropic deps anywhere. This is the single biggest gap — the whole "100+ agents, orchestrator, debate" story is unbuilt. You have the verification half of the design, not the proposal half.

2. **No validate/debate stage.** MDASH's "debater personas argue for/against to kill false positives" has no analog. `GraphVerifier` is a structural in-degree check, not adversarial validation.

3. **No dynamic proof-of-concept.** MDASH's headline "proof" node generates an actual crashing input. Your findings are 100% static — no PoC, no execution, no `--prove`. This is what separates "noisy alerts" from "proven actionable" in their pitch.

4. **No SARIF output.** Zero (`grep -ri sarif` is empty). MDASH emits SARIF → GitHub Advanced Security. This is the cheapest high-value gap to close.

5. **No HTML report** for management, **no `fix`/remediate command**, **no PR creation**. All three are in the MDASH demo; none exist here.

6. **No memory-safety templates.** Your `TemplateKind`s are `MissingDominatorCheck`, `ReachesWithoutSanitizer`, `MustFollow`, `DeviantFrequency` (`vuln/mod.rs:68`). The MDASH demo bugs are UAF / double-free / OOB-read / RCE — none have a template. Worse, **`MustFollow` and `DeviantFrequency` are defined but never emitted** by `vuln_report` (`analyze.rs:3894`) — dead templates that could model free-without-use and stale-map-OOB.

7. **No threat-model "prepare" stage** and **no benchmark harness** (CyberGym-style reproduce-real-vulns recall numbers).

8. Concurrency lint is wired **Rust-only** in `vuln_report` (`analyze.rs:3944`).

### Highest-leverage first move
SARIF output (#4) + emitting `MustFollow` (#6) are small and unlock the GitHub Advanced Security story immediately. The real moat-closer is #1: implement a `PredicateClassifier` in the MCP/agent layer (which has model access) and route it through the already-built `GraphVerifier` — that turns the existing dead trust-boundary into the "model proposes, graph proves" loop MDASH sells. Donate > cs > arXiv:2506.02548 Help | Advanced Search All fields Title Author Abstract Comments Journal reference ACM classification MSC classification Report number arXiv identifier DOI ORCID arXiv author ID Help pages Full text Search GO quick links Login Help Pages About -- Computer Science Cryptography and Security arXiv:2506.02548 (cs) [Submitted on 3 Jun 2025 (v1), last revised 24 Mar 2026 (this version, v3)] Title:CyberGym: Evaluating AI Agents' Real-World Cybersecurity Capabilities at Scale Authors:Zhun Wang, Tianneng Shi, Jingxuan He, Matthew Cai, Jialin Zhang, Dawn Song View a PDF of the paper titled CyberGym: Evaluating AI Agents' Real-World Cybersecurity Capabilities at Scale, by Zhun Wang and 5 other authors View PDF Abstract:AI agents have significant potential to reshape cybersecurity, making a thorough assessment of their capabilities critical. However, existing evaluations fall short, because they are based on small-scale benchmarks and only measure static outcomes, failing to capture the full, dynamic range of real-world security challenges. To address these limitations, we introduce CyberGym, a large-scale benchmark featuring 1,507 real-world vulnerabilities across 188 software projects. Adjustable to different vulnerability analysis settings, CyberGym primarily tasks agents with generating a proof-of-concept test that reproduces a vulnerability, given only its text description and the corresponding codebase. Our extensive evaluation highlights that CyberGym effectively differentiates agents' and models' cybersecurity capabilities. Even the top-performing combinations only achieve a ~20% success rate, demonstrating the overall difficulty of CyberGym. Beyond static benchmarking, we show that CyberGym leads to the discovery of 34 zero-day vulnerabilities and 18 historically incomplete patches. These results underscore that CyberGym is not only a robust benchmark for measuring AI's progress in cybersecurity but also a platform for creating direct, real-world security impact. First, LLM-generated vulnerability reports suffer from high false positive rates and lack reproducible verification. Second, existing LLM-based approaches use suboptimal granularities for vulnerability localization: function-level analysis overlooks bugs when context becomes extensive, while line-level analysis lacks sufficient context. Third, existing approaches have difficulty reasoning about vulnerabilities with complex cross-function dependencies and triggering conditions. We present FuzzingBrain V2, a multi-agent system that addresses these gaps through four key contributions: (1) fully automated vulnerability analysis built on Google's OSS-Fuzz, ensuring all reported vulnerabilities are fuzzer-reproducible; (2) Suspicious Point, a novel control-flow-based abstraction for precise vulnerability localization at the optimal granularity; (3) logic-driven hierarchical function analysis with dual-layer fuzzing enhancing function coverage under resource constraints; (4) MCP-based static and dynamic analysis tools with context engineering enhancing complex vulnerability reasoning. On the AIxCC 2025 Final Competition C/C++ dataset, FuzzingBrain V2 achieved 90% detection rate (36 of 40 vulnerabilities). In real-world deployment, FuzzingBrain V2 discovered 29 zero-day vulnerabilities across 12 open-source projects, all confirmed and fixed by maintainers, with 2 assigned CVE IDs. ## What the evidence actually supports

### Dynamic PoC generation, exploit reproduction, and OSS-Fuzz integration (part 2)

Two concrete, relevant systems appear:

- **OSS-Fuzz + LLM vulnerability fixing** — "Fixing Security Vulnerabilities with AI in OSS-Fuzz" (arXiv:2411.03346) [2]. This work sits at the intersection you asked about: fuzz campaigns conduct a "biased random search over the domain of program input[s]," and the authors integrate LLM-based remediation into the OSS-Fuzz pipeline [2]. It is relevant both to exploit reproduction (fuzzing produces crashing inputs / PoCs) and to the downstream repair loop. Authors include Google OSS-Fuzz maintainers (Dongge Liu, Abhishek Arya, Oliver Chang) and academic security researchers (Abhik Roychoudhury) [2].

- **CyberGym** — "CyberGym: Evaluating AI Agents' Real-World Cybersecurity Capabilities at Scale" (arXiv:2506.02548) [2]. This is the most directly relevant benchmark in the evidence. It explicitly targets evaluating AI agents' real-world cybersecurity capabilities "at scale," and motivates itself by noting that "existing evaluations fall short, because they are based on small-scale" datasets [2]. It is from Dawn Song's Berkeley group (Zhun Wang, Tianneng Shi, Jingxuan He, et al.) [2]. This is the natural benchmark to situate any MDASH-style harness against, though the evidence excerpt does not give recall / false-positive numbers.

Donate > cs > arXiv:2509.11523 Help | Advanced Search All fields Title Author Abstract Comments Journal reference ACM classification MSC classification Report number arXiv identifier DOI ORCID arXiv author ID Help pages Full text Search GO quick links Login Help Pages About -- Computer Science Software Engineering arXiv:2509.11523 (cs) [Submitted on 15 Sep 2025] Title:VulAgent: Hypothesis-Validation based Multi-Agent Vulnerability Detection Authors:Ziliang Wang, Ge Li, Jia Li, Hao Zhu, Zhi Jin View a PDF of the paper titled VulAgent: Hypothesis-Validation based Multi-Agent Vulnerability Detection, by Ziliang Wang and 4 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract:The application of language models to project-level vulnerability detection remains challenging, owing to the dual requirement of accurately localizing security-sensitive code and correctly correlating and reasoning over complex program context. We present VulAgent, a multi-agent vulnerability detection framework based on hypothesis validation. Our design is inspired by how human auditors review code: when noticing a sensitive operation, they form a hypothesis about a possible vulnerability, consider potential trigger paths, and then verify the hypothesis against the surrounding context. VulAgent implements a semantics-sensitive, multi-view detection pipeline: specialized agents, each aligned to a specific analysis perspective (e.g., memory, authorization), collaboratively surface and precisely localize sensitive code sites with higher coverage. Building on this, VulAgent adopts a hypothesis-validation paradigm: for each vulnerability report, it builds hypothesis conditions and a trigger path, steering the LLM to target the relevant program context and defensive checks during verification, which reduces false positives. On average across the two datasets, VulAgent improves overall accuracy by 6.6%, increases the correct identification rate of vulnerable--fixed code pairs by up to 450% (246% on average), and reduces the false positive rate by about 36% compared with state-of-the-art LLM-based baselines. Musliner, Jeff Huang, Jon Silliman, Mikel Mcdaniel, Jefferson Casavant, Isaac Goldthwaite, Nicholas Vidovich, Matthew Lehman, Taesoo Kim View a PDF of the paper titled SoK: DARPA's AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC): Competition Design, Architectures, and Lessons Learned, by Cen Zhang and 20 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract:DARPA's AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC, 2023--2025) is the largest competition to date for building fully autonomous cyber reasoning systems (CRSs) that leverage recent advances in AI -- particularly large language models (LLMs) -- to discover and remediate vulnerabilities in real-world open-source software. This paper presents the first systematic analysis of AIxCC. Drawing on design documents, source code, execution traces, and discussions with organizers and competing teams, we examine the competition's structure and key design decisions, characterize the architectural approaches of finalist CRSs, and analyze competition results beyond the final scoreboard. Donate > cs > arXiv:2604.07624 Help | Advanced Search All fields Title Author Abstract Comments Journal reference ACM classification MSC classification Report number arXiv identifier DOI ORCID arXiv author ID Help pages Full text Search GO quick links Login Help Pages About -- Computer Science Software Engineering arXiv:2604.07624 (cs) [Submitted on 8 Apr 2026] Title:Program Analysis Guided LLM Agent for Proof-of-Concept Generation Authors:Achintya Desai, Md Shafiuzzaman, Wenbo Guo, Tevfik Bultan View a PDF of the paper titled Program Analysis Guided LLM Agent for Proof-of-Concept Generation, by Achintya Desai and 3 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract:Software developers frequently receive vulnerability reports that require them to reproduce the vulnerability in a reliable manner by generating a proof-of-concept (PoC) input that triggers it. Given the source code for a software project and a specific code location for a potential vulnerability, automatically generating a PoC for the given vulnerability has been a challenging research problem. Symbolic execution and fuzzing techniques require expert guidance and manual steps and face scalability challenges for PoC generation. Although recent advances in LLMs have increased the level of automation and scalability, the success rate of PoC generation with LLMs remains quite low. In this paper, we present a novel approach called Program Analysis Guided proof of concept generation agENT (PAGENT) that is scalable and significantly improves the success rate of automated PoC generation compared to prior results. PAGENT integrates lightweight and rule-based static analysis phases for providing static analysis guidance and sanitizer-based profiling and coverage information for providing dynamic analysis guidance with a PoC generation agent. Our experiments demonstrate that the resulting hybrid approach significantly outperforms the prior top-performing agentic approach by 132% for the PoC generation task. [2606.03453] FORGE: Multi-Agent Graduated Exploitation and Detection Engineering Skip to main content Learn about arXiv becoming an independent nonprofit. We gratefully acknowledge support from the Simons Foundation, member institutions, and all contributors. Donate > cs > arXiv:2606.03453 Help | Advanced Search All fields Title Author Abstract Comments Journal reference ACM classification MSC classification Report number arXiv identifier DOI ORCID arXiv author ID Help pages Full text Search GO quick links Login Help Pages About -- Computer Science Cryptography and Security arXiv:2606.03453 (cs) [Submitted on 2 Jun 2026] Title:FORGE: Multi-Agent Graduated Exploitation and Detection Engineering Authors:Farooq Shaikh View a PDF of the paper titled FORGE: Multi-Agent Graduated Exploitation and Detection Engineering, by Farooq Shaikh View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract:Vulnerability disclosure volumes now far exceed organizational assessment capacity, yet three adjacent research communities (proof-of-concept generation, vulnerability prioritization, and detection rule engineering) operate largely in isolation. Existing automated exploit generation systems report binary pass/fail outcomes, discarding partial progress and producing no signal for the other two communities. This paper presents FORGE, a multi-agent system that bridges these three silos through graduated exploitation depth. Five specialized agents (Intel, Generator, Planner, Exploit, and Detector) execute in a fixed pipeline that (1) generates targeted vulnerable applications from CVE metadata, (2) conducts coached, multi-turn exploitation assessed by an LLM-primary oracle on a four-level taxonomy (L0: no evidence through L3: full compromise), and (3) produces Sigma and Snort detection rules grounded in OpenTelemetry exploitation traces. Graduated depth is the bridging mechanism: deeper exploitation yields richer behavioral traces for detection engineering, while depth data across scoring bands provides ground truth for prioritization validation. A tiered knowledge architecture accumulates intelligence across assessments, transferring build and exploitation experience to subsequent CVEs. Evaluation on 603 CVEs from the CVE-GENIE dataset achieves 67.8% end-to-end L1+ exploitation at USD 1.50 per CVE across eight languages and 187 CWE types. Exploitation rates remain near 68% regardless of EPSS or CVSS band, indicating that pattern-level reachability is orthogonal to metadata-based prioritization. Detection rules from L2+ exploitation achieve significantly higher span-normalized grounding than L1-derived rules (p=0.035), and 93.4% of generated Snort rules produce zero false positives against a synthetic benign corpus. Comments: 18 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at the AgentCy Workshop at the 21st International Conference on Availability, Reliability and Security (ARES 2026). Here's the research landscape around MDASH, grounded in the authoritative Microsoft source plus the directly-analogous academic systems I pulled.

## What MDASH actually is (authoritative)
Per Microsoft Security's blog (May 12 2026) and the CyberOne partner writeup: **MDASH = Multi-Model Agentic Scanning Harness**, built by Microsoft's **Autonomous Code Security team**. Not one model — an **orchestrator coordinating 100+ specialized agents** over a configurable ensemble (heavy reasoning models for hard analysis, light models for high-volume scanning). Pipeline: **prepare (threat model) → scan (auditor agents) → validate (debater personas argue for/against to kill false positives) → proof (dynamic PoC)**. Reported numbers: **96.55% CyberGym** (up from 88.45% in 3 weeks), **16 zero-days** in Windows networking/auth (4 critical RCE), **100% recall** on confirmed MSRC cases, **0 false positives** against 21 planted vulns. Outputs SARIF + HTML, with `Defender fix` and PR creation.

## The academic systems that mirror each MDASH piece

**Multi-agent + debate validation (the "validate" stage):**
- **VulAgent** (arXiv:2509.11523) — the closest analog to MDASH's design. "Hypothesis-validation" modeled on human auditors: specialized agents per perspective (memory, authorization) localize sites, then build *hypothesis conditions + a trigger path* to verify against defensive checks. **−36% false-positive rate, +246% avg correct vuln/fixed-pair identification** vs SOTA LLM baselines. This is essentially MDASH's auditor→debater loop, published.
- **PhishDebate** (2506.15656) and **SWE-Debate** (2507.23348) — the multi-agent *debate* primitive MDASH uses for false-positive suppression, in adjacent domains.

**Dynamic PoC / "proof" stage (the part codegraph entirely lacks):**
- **PAGENT** (arXiv:2604.07624) — most relevant to your engine: a PoC agent *guided by lightweight static analysis + sanitizer profiling/coverage*. Hybrid static-guidance approach **+132%** over the prior best agentic PoC method. This is the template for wiring your existing taint/reachability graph into a proof generator.
- **FuzzingBrain V2** (2605.21779) — multi-agent on OSS-Fuzz so every report is fuzzer-reproducible; "Suspicious Point" control-flow abstraction; **90% on AIxCC 2025 C/C++ (36/40)**, 29 zero-days, 2 CVEs.
- **FaultLine** (2507.15241), **PoCGen** (2506.04962, npm), **Fang/Kang line**: *LLM agents autonomously exploit one-day* (2404.08144) and *teams exploit zero-day* (2406.01637).

**Benchmarks & the competition context:**
- **CyberGym** (arXiv:2506.02548, Dawn Song/Berkeley) — MDASH's headline benchmark: 1,507 real vulns across 188 projects; agent must generate a reproducing PoC from description + codebase. Even top combos hit only ~20% baseline → MDASH's 96.55% is the contested claim.
- **AIxCC SoK** (arXiv:2602.07666, USENIX Sec 2026) — systematization of DARPA's AI Cyber Challenge cyber-reasoning systems; the definitive "what actually drove autonomous CRS performance" reference. Plus **OSS-CRS** (2603.08566) liberating those CRSs for real OSS.
- **FORGE** (2606.03453) — 5 fixed agents (Intel/Generator/Planner/Exploit/Detector), **graduated** L0–L3 exploitation depth instead of binary pass/fail; 67.8% L1+ on 603 CVEs at $1.50/CVE; 93.4% of generated Snort rules zero-FP.

## How this maps to your gap list
- **VulAgent's hypothesis→trigger-path validation** ≈ your unbuilt `GraphVerifier` (`classify.rs:60`, 0 callers). The published win is the model *proposes*, structured verification *prunes* — exactly your "model proposes, graph proves" design that's currently dead code.
- **PAGENT** proves the highest-leverage move: your interprocedural taint + reachability (`scan_unsanitized_flows`, points-to oracle) is precisely the "static guidance" that lifts PoC success +132%. You have the substrate; you lack the agent loop.
- **The property/signature split** you already articulate in `vuln/mod.rs:1-26` (fixed `TemplateKind`s + inferred sinks/guards) is the same separation these systems are converging on — you're architecturally aligned, just missing the LLM-proposal + dynamic-proof halves.
